questions.scenario:
The DevPolaris release pipeline has pushed an image and registered a task definition, but the service has not completed the same release path.

Pushed image is devpolaris-orders-api:2026-05-02.5. Registered task definition is devpolaris-orders-api:44.

ECS service still shows: TaskDefinition is devpolaris-orders-api:43. Deployments is PRIMARY revision 43 rollout COMPLETED.

What has not happened yet?

options.text: Register another task definition revision with the same image and retry the update before checking why the service still references revision 41.
Update the service to revision 42 because it is the newest revision named in the deployment runbook, even though the pipeline registered revision 44.
The ECS service has not been updated to run task definition revision 44.
Force a new deployment of revision 41 because ECS can resolve the newer image tag without changing the task definition.

questions.explanation:
A service update is the moment ECS is told which recipe to run. Pushing an image and registering a task definition are necessary steps, but they do not move customer traffic by themselves. The service still points at revision 43.

Learn more in [Amazon ECS rolling deployments](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/deployment-type-ecs.html).

questions.scenario:
A replacement ECS task reaches RUNNING during an orders API deployment, but its readiness check depends on a successful database startup check. The release captain sees:

```text
target=10.0.43.27:3000 state=unhealthy
reason=Target.ResponseCodeMismatch response_code=500
release=2026-05-02.5 database=startup_check_failed health=not_ready
```

What must happen before the task is allowed to serve production traffic?

options.text: Mark the target healthy manually because RUNNING is stronger evidence than health checks.
Stop the old healthy tasks so users are forced onto the new version.
Increase the ALB success-code range to accept 500 for production health.
Keep traffic away from the task and fix the readiness or config failure in a new revision or rollback.

questions.explanation:
RUNNING means the container process exists, but target health decides whether the ALB should trust it. The log explains why readiness failed. Sending traffic anyway would turn a protected failure into a user-facing failure.

Learn more in [Application Load Balancer target health](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/latest/application/target-group-health-checks.html).

questions.scenario:
The DevPolaris release captain is reviewing a production change and its recorded rollback evidence.

Current deployment is devpolaris-orders-api:42. Current image digest is sha256:bf91. Rollback_target is devpolaris-orders-api:41. Revision 41 evidence is target_health 2 healthy, smoke test passed at 09:55.

Current failure: revision 42 listened on 8080 while target group checks 3000

What is the likely rollback action?

options.text: Update the ECS service back to task definition revision 41, while recording why revision 42 failed.
Force another deployment of revision 43 with a longer health-check grace period because revision 42 may recover during replacement.
Update back to revision 42 because it is the current revision and preserve revision 41 only as documentation.
Create revision 44 from the current definition and point it at the previous image tag rather than using the recorded healthy revision.

questions.explanation:
The record names a previous known-good runtime recipe. Rolling back means telling the service to run that recipe again. The team should also keep the failure reason so revision 43 fixes the port contract instead of repeating it.

Learn more in [Amazon ECS rolling deployments](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/deployment-type-ecs.html).

questions.scenario:
The DevPolaris release captain must decide whether replacement ECS tasks have earned production traffic. The captain collects evidence from the service, target group, and application.

Service event is registered targets in orders-api-tg. Target health is 10.0.42.18:3000 healthy, 10.0.43.27:3000 healthy. Startup log is release=2026-05-02.5 port=3000 health=ready. Smoke test is GET /v1/orders/health-check-order 200.

Which evidence belongs in the release note?

options.text: Startup logs from the expected release showing config and readiness.
The image digest exists in ECR and the service deployment reports `COMPLETED`, without checking target health.
Target health for the new task IPs on the expected port.
A smoke test or request metric after traffic moved.
The new tasks have reached RUNNING, but the target group still reports `initial` during health evaluation.

questions.explanation:
New tasks earn traffic through several checks: service registration, target health, app logs, and a small user-path proof. The image tag is necessary earlier, but it does not prove the service accepted or served the revision.

Learn more in [Application Load Balancer target health](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/elasticloadbalancing/latest/application/target-group-health-checks.html).

questions.scenario:
Checkout errors rose after a DevPolaris release, and the release captain is considering an ECS capacity increase. Before scaling, the captain reviews the same 15-minute window across the serving and database layers.

ALB requests is 118 per minute, normal. Target 5xx is rising after 10:42. ECS is desired 4, running 4, CPU 31 percent, memory 48 percent. RDS connections is 96 percent of the pool. Checkout write latency is high.

What is the safest first scaling decision?

options.text: Increase desired count from 3 to 6 because request volume rose, then check whether database latency falls.
Do not add more API tasks yet; reduce pressure and inspect database connections or the change that increased database work.
Increase task CPU because aggregate utilization is low but target response time is high.
Pause scaling changes and investigate the RDS connection and latency movement that aligns with the 5xx window.

questions.explanation:
The API has spare CPU while the database is under pressure. More API tasks may create more connections and make the bottleneck worse. Scaling should follow the saturated layer, not the first service name in the incident.

Learn more in [Amazon ECS services](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/ecs_services.html).

questions.scenario:
Checkout logs during a provider slowdown:

```text
POST /v1/orders orderId=ord_1042 database=create_ok latency_ms=84
receipt provider call latency_ms=6100
checkout response latency_ms=6420
customer retries: rising
```

What design change best fits the article?

options.text: Increase the checkout timeout so the receipt call can finish synchronously during normal dependency latency.
Publish receipt work to SQS before the order transaction commits so checkout can return even if the order write later fails.
Invoke Lambda asynchronously without durable queueing because failed receipt sends can be reconstructed from API logs.
Move receipt email to a queue-backed worker so checkout can return after the order is safely created and the message is sent.

questions.explanation:
Receipt email is slow side work that does not need to block order creation. A queue gives the system a buffer and a clearer failure boundary. The API should log that it created the order and enqueued the receipt job.

Learn more in [Amazon ECS services](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/ecs_services.html).

questions.scenario:
The DevPolaris receipt worker is sending duplicate emails while its downstream provider is timing out. The team needs an incident control that protects customers and preserves recoverable work.

Worker release is 2026-05-02.9. Idempotency log is missing key for orderId ord_1042. Queue visible messages is 3,200. Checkout API is healthy. Customer impact is duplicate receipts, orders still created correctly.

Which control best buys time?

options.text: Pause or reduce the receipt worker desired count while preserving queued messages and preparing a fix or rollback.
Reduce worker concurrency and retain the queue while the downstream dependency recovers.
Set a short message retention period so backlog cannot grow beyond the incident window.
Purge only messages that have exceeded the business retry window after capturing identifiers and approving the data-loss decision.

questions.explanation:
The damage is in the worker side effect, not the checkout API. Pausing or reducing consumers can stop more duplicate emails while keeping the queue as the work record. It is smaller and easier to reverse than disabling all checkout.

Learn more in [Amazon ECS services](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonECS/latest/developerguide/ecs_services.html).
